did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates an ensemble model combining deep convolutional neural networks (CNN) and stacked bidirectional long short-term memory (BLSTM) networks for skin disease classification. The method extracts deep features from skin disease images and uses the BLSTM network to learn sequential features, achieving a mean accuracy of 91.73% on skin disease image datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. The abstract and introduction describe the proposal of a novel hybrid classification method combining deep convolutional neural networks and stacked bidirectional long short-term memory (BLSTM) networks. The paper details the methodology, dataset usage (including a hospital-collected dataset and HAM10000), experimental setup, and results (achieving 91.73% mean accuracy), which are characteristic of original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: An ensemble model of convolution and recurrent neural network for skin disease classification
journal: International Journal of Imaging Systems and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid classification model combining deep convolutional neural networks (CNN) and stacked bidirectional long short-term memory (BLSTM) networks for skin disease classification. The method extracts deep features from skin disease images and utilizes a dual BLSTM network to learn sequential features, which are then processed through max-pooling and a fully connected layer for classification. The authors evaluated the model's generalization capability using two datasets: a hospital-collected dataset from Wuhan and the public HAM10000 benchmark dataset. The experimental setup involved training on 80% of the data and testing on the remaining 20%, with 5% of the training data reserved for validation. The proposed ensemble model achieved a mean accuracy of 91.73%, demonstrating significant improvements over state-of-the-art skin disease classification methods.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease diagnostic categories (e.g., Acnes, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin disease classification'. The model uses a softmax function to classify skin disease images into discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrices for multiple classes (multiclass classification) on the hospital-collected dataset and the HAM10000 benchmark dataset.

datasets_used.name: Hospital-collected dataset from Wuhan
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of skin disease images collected from a hospital in Wuhan, China. It contains 7 diagnostic categories.

use.training: Used for training the model (80% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation (5% of the training data split).
use.testing: Used for testing (20% of the dataset).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset consisting of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of 5600 images.

use.training: Used for training the model (80% of the selected 5600 images).
use.validation: Used for validation (5% of the training data split).
use.testing: Used for testing (20% of the selected 5600 images).
use.other: Used to improve the generalization capability of the proposed method.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To avoid run time error and obtain a meaningful result by computing the average accuracy across folds.
To summarize prediction results, calculate sensitivity (recall) and specificity, and identify types of misclassifications for each class.
To graphically demonstrate the classification skill of the proposed method by plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) against False Positive Rate (FPR).
To prevent serious over-fitting during the training process, particularly on smaller down-sampling sizes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares performance using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC without statistical significance tests or p-values.

Hospital-collected dataset (Our dataset).notes: Per-class results for the proposed method: Acnes (Acc: 93.37, Sens: 99.37), Basal cell carcinoma (Acc: 91.84, Sens: 99.37), Blackheads (Acc: 91.17, Sens: 97.97), Dark circles (Acc: 91.97, Sens: 97.50), Melanoma (Acc: 94.31, Sens: 98.96), Spots (Acc: 92.29, Sens: 98.78), Tinea (Acc: 87.89, Sens: 97.45). Validation accuracy for 1-BLSTM layer was 89.08% and for 2-BLSTM layers was 91.73%.

HAM10000 benchmark dataset.notes: Per-class results for the proposed method: Melanoma (Acc: 98.66, Sens: 97.05), Melanocytic nevi (Acc: 99.37, Sens: 98.21), Basal cell carcinoma (Acc: 90.70, Sens: 96.05), Actinic keratosis (Acc: 96.00, Sens: 96.64), Benign keratosis (Acc: 63.50, Sens: 99.88), Dermatofibroma (Acc: 95.41, Sens: 92.84), Vascular lesions (Acc: 74.67, Sens: 99.54).

Comparison with traditional methods (Agg.notes: The proposed method achieved the best mean accuracy of 91.73% compared to state-of-the-art methods.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble model of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Stacked Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BLSTM)

cnn_structure.layers: Two parallel networks: one with two convolution layers (decoder) and another with three convolution layers with two max-pooling and two linear layers.

blstm_structure.mechanism: Dual BLSTM through max-pooling, forward and backward LSTM hidden states of both the feature matrix and its transpose concatenated for inputting into a dense FC layer.

training_details.data_split: 80% training, 20% testing, 5% of training data for validation
training_details.hyperparameter_optimization: Grid search for filter number, filter size, pool size, batch size, and learning rate; limited exploration due to computational resources.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed method (Ensemble model of Convolutional Neural Network and Stacked Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during preprocessing of input skin disease images.
Applied with a range during preprocessing.
Images resized to dimension 100 (height and width) during preprocessing.
Positive and negative samples were made into equal sizes to avoid asymmetry problem in the hospital-collected dataset.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble model combining deep convolutional neural networks (CNN) and stacked bidirectional long short-term memory (BLSTM) networks achieved the best mean accuracy of 91.73% on the hospital-collected dataset and 89.47% on the HAM10000 benchmark dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
The proposed method demonstrated significant improvements in skin disease classification compared to existing traditional methods and other deep learning models (CNN, CNN-LSTM, CNN-GRU, CNN-BLSTM).
The BLSTM model outperformed LSTM and GRU models, particularly due to its ability to retain associated information in both directions without requiring prior knowledge or pre-designing features.
The model effectively handles the vanishing or exploding loss gradient problem often faced by classical methods and does not require benchmark datasets, as it generates embeddings from input data.
Over-fitting remains a major problem for large down-sampling sizes, although early-stopping strategies prevented severe over-fitting on smaller down-sampling sizes.
Performance can be improved through detailed hyperparameter optimization and the use of additional computational resources.
The proposed method reduces classification accuracy issues for highly similar malignant and benign lesion images and irrelevant feature selection by integrating CNN and BLSTM advantages.
The method has good generalization ability for various classification tasks and can be applied to other disease classification problems.
important_remarks: The private hospital-collected dataset from Wuhan is extremely small (total N=245, 35 images per class), raising significant concerns about statistical power and generalizability.

The HAM10000 benchmark evaluation used a highly restricted subset of only 5,600 images with perfectly balanced classes (900 images per class), which does not reflect the dataset's known severe class imbalance and may inflate performance estimates.
The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical robustness.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) was reported to validate the claimed superiority over state-of-the-art methods.
Hyperparameter optimization was limited by computational resources, suggesting the reported results may not represent the model's full potential.
The paper acknowledges that overfitting remains a problem for large down-sampling sizes, indicating a potential limitation in handling complex feature spaces.
Performance on specific classes in the HAM10000 dataset was notably weak (e.g., Benign keratosis accuracy 63.50%, Vascular lesions accuracy 74.67%), highlighting class-specific vulnerabilities.
